whatThisIs: Compute defibrillation energy doses by patient size. For adults, the AHA recommendation is the device-specific biphasic dose (typically 120-200 J initial, escalating per the manufacturer) for shockable rhythms in cardiac arrest. For pediatric patients, the weight-based dose is 2 J/kg for the first shock, 4 J/kg for subsequent shocks, with consideration of doses up to 10 J/kg or the adult dose (whichever is lower) for refractory VF/pulseless VT. Sophie Well also reports the appropriate synchronized cardioversion dose (0.5-1 J/kg) for unstable narrow-complex tachyarrhythmias.
whenToUse: Use this when preparing for or running a pediatric resuscitation and you need the weight-based joule numbers without flipping through the PALS card, when verifying the dose written on a code-cart drug-and-dose sheet, or when an educator is building a PALS or ACLS competency module. Defibrillation is one of the only interventions in cardiac arrest with a strong evidence base for improving outcome; getting the dose right and the timing right matter more than any other rhythm-management decision.
